coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a dual sensor for the selective determination of d-penicillamine and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Gunjal, Datta B.
Gore, Anil H.
Naik, Vaibhav M.
Pawar, Samadhan P.
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Shejwal, Rajendra V.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: Dual mode Nanosensors gained tremendous interest in recent years because of their facile synthesis and dual applications. Herein we have synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from Mahogany fruit shell by chemical oxidation method. The as prepared CDs exhibited selective and sensitive quenching of fluorescence by Fe3+ and also accompanied by a dramatic increase in absorption intensity. Hence these two processes led to fabricate CDs-Fe3+ system as a dual probe. d-Penicillamine (D-PA) has much affinity toward Fe3+ which resulted in recovery of almost 75% fluorescence intensity and decrease in absorption of the CDs-Fe3+ system. Thus, this tendency has been exploited for the selective detection of D-PA by both Spectrofluorimetrically and UV-Visible spectroscopically and showed a wide linear range of detection 0-48 μg mL−1 and 0-40 μg mL−1 respectively. This developed probe offered low cost, high selectivity, repeatability, facile operation and excellent recovery ratio in detection of D-PA in pharmaceutical samples. Moreover, biological applications CDs were investigated using Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain with confocal microscopy. We found that CDs were a biocompatible and ideal candidate for differential staining of yeast cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Dual sensor
UV-Visible and spectrofluorimetrically
d-penicillamine detection
Cell imaging
Biocompatibility

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of D-PA in pharmaceutical sample
CDs-Fe3+ system based “turn-on” fluorescence detection of D-PA
CDs-Fe3+ system based “turn-off” UV-Visible detection of D-PA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: One of the author DBG is grateful to the University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for providing financial assistance in the form of UGC-NET-JRF scheme ( F.17-11/2008 (SA-I). We are also thankful to the Shivaji University, Kolhapur for providing all necessary facilities for this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Normalized an overlapping absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution. b) Excitation dependent emission spectrum of CDs.
A) TEM image and size distribution pattern (inset) B) XRD pattern of as prepared CDs.
a) Effect of pH b) Effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of CDs.
Fluorescent intensity quenching of CDs by Fe3+ and subsequent restoration by D-PA.
Absorption spectra of CDs, CDs-Fe3+, CDs/Fe3+/D-PA, Fe3+/D-PA, CDs/D-PA systems.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-Fe3+ system with different concentration of D-PA (0–80 μg mL-1).
Response of F/F0 of CD-Fe3+ system with concentration of D-PA.
Effect of co-existing biomolecules, cations and anions higher in concentration compared to the D-PA on the emission intensity of CD-Fe3+ system.
Absorption spectra of CD-Fe3+ system with various concentration of D-PA.
Calibration curve of (A0-A) values of CD-Fe3+ system with concentration of D-PA.
Absorbance response CD-Fe3+ system in presence of 40 μg mL−1 of various compounds and ions.
Zeta potential of CDs, CDs/Fe3+ and CDs/Fe3+/D-PA.
Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of S. cerevisiae incubated with CDs for 3 h.
The growth curves of S. cerevisiae with different concentrations of CDs. The values are plotted as mean ± SD (n = 3).
Quantification of D-PA in real sample.

row.entry:
Amount of D-PA in tablet (μg mL−1)
Cilamin-250 mg capsule

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a dual sensor for the selective determination of d-penicillamine and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416 004, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lal Bahadur Shastri College of Arts, Science & Commerce, Satara 415002, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lal Bahadur Shastri College of Arts, Science & Commerce

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Rajarshi Chhatrapati Sahu College, Kolhapur 416005, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry Rajarshi Chhatrapati Sahu College

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual mode Nanosensors gained tremendous interest in recent years because of their facile synthesis and dual applications. Herein we have synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from Mahogany fruit shell by chemical oxidation method. The as prepared CDs exhibited selective and sensitive quenching of fluorescence by Fe3+ and also accompanied by a dramatic increase in absorption intensity. Hence these two processes led to fabricate CDs-Fe3+ system as a dual probe. d-Penicillamine (D-PA) has much affinity toward Fe3+ which resulted in recovery of almost 75% fluorescence intensity and decrease in absorption of the CDs-Fe3+ system. Thus, this tendency has been exploited for the selective detection of D-PA by both Spectrofluorimetrically and UV-Visible spectroscopically and showed a wide linear range of detection 0-48 μg mL−1 and 0-40 μg mL−1 respectively. This developed probe offered low cost, high selectivity, repeatability, facile operation and excellent recovery ratio in detection of D-PA in pharmaceutical samples. Moreover, biological applications CDs were investigated using Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain with confocal microscopy. We found that CDs were a biocompatible and ideal candidate for differential staining of yeast cells.

Schematic illustration of Carbon dots based dual sensor for the selective determination of d-penicillamine.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Penicillamine (3,3-dimethylcysteine) is a hydrolytic degradation product of penicillin. It has an alpha-amine, a carboxyl and sulfhydryl functional groups which largely determine its pharmacological effects. It is low molecular weight, pharmaceutical significant sulfur containing chiral amino acid which has enticed in recent years owing to their therapeutic applications and having mighty chelating ability with heavy metal ions [1]. Generally, it has D and L enantiomeric form which has different biological and toxicological properties. Levorotatory Penicillamine (L-PA) isomer is a pyridoxine antagonist and extremely toxic and even small dose can causes adverse effects while d-Penicillamine (D-PA) is a clinically useful isomer [2]. D-PA has been widely used as a therapeutic agent in various diseases; one of them is a Wilson's disease, a genetic disease that results in excessive copper deposits in the body tissues [3,4]. It is also used as antirheumatic drug to treat rheumatoid arthritis [5]. Along with this, D-PA finds applications as a potential drug in scleroderma, primary biliary cirrhosis, cystinuria, liver disease and heavy metal poisoning [6]. It is also quickly absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract and is rapidly oxidized to various disulphide forms [7]. However, this sulfhydryl drug also induces numerous side-effects, including myasthenia gravis, ageusia or dysgeusia rash, thrombocytopenia, pemphigus, agranulocytosis, polymyositis, proteinuria, or hypersensitivity nephritic syndrome [2,8,9]. Thus D-PA not only acts as a biological but also pharmaceutical significant medication concerned to human health. Hence, it is essential to monitor the concentrations of these compounds in biological fluids as well as in pharmaceuticals samples.

Till date, there are many attempts have been carried out for the determination of D-PA in pharmaceutical preparation and biological samples by using various analytical methods. These are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10,11], colorimetry, Spectrophotometry [12,13], voltammetry/electrochemical sensor [14,15], flow injection analysis [16,17], capillary electrophoresis [18], chemiluminescence [19]and electrochemistry [20]. Although these methods found to be suitable for quantification of D-PA, some of them undergo interference from the pharmaceutical or biological matrix while others are time consuming and require expensive equipment and, as a result, are not suitable for routine analysis in many laboratories. Therefore, there is a need to address the limitations of these methods with the development of simple, rapid and sensitive and selective method for determination of D-PA in pharmaceutical samples. Now a days the fluorescent based sensors have attracted tremendous attention of all over the world due to their operational simplicity and excellent sensitivity [21].

Recently luminescent carbon dots (CDs), a new class of nanoparticles, have tempted owing to their splendid properties such as less-toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, ease of surface modification and tunable luminescence, chemical inertness and stability, over the traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots [22–24]. These properties make CDs as precious and environmental benign fluorescent material in bio-imaging [25,26], optoelectronics [27], drug delivery [28,29], biological labeling, and sensing [30–32]. The CDs particularly shows remarkable applications in the field of chemical and biological sensing. The chemical sensing based on CDs mainly includes the detections of diverse metal ions and inorganic anions, such as Fe3+, Cr4+, Hg2+ [33], Pb2+ [34], Ag+, Cu2+ [35],C2O4 2−, PO4 3−, CN−, F−, ClO− and I−.While CDs also played an admirable role for the detection of small biomolecules such as dopamine, ascorbic acid, glucose and Amoxicillin [36].

In our previous work, firstly we developed a colorimetric strategy for the detection of D-PA by using silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [37]. Furthermore, we demonstrated Cds quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the quantification of D-PA from pharmaceutical formulation based on fluorescent enhancement [38]. Up to date, some scientific reports for the determination of D-PA based on CDs have been published. Recently, Yusheng et al. used CDs, based on fluorescence recovery experiment, as a fluorescent sensor for the measurement of D-PA [39]; Naghdi et al. have synthesized carbon dots from chitin nanofiber and employed ‘off-on’ strategy for the detection of D-PA [40]. While Mahajan et al. performed a novel organic nanoparticles based fluorescent probe for the measurement of D-PA [41]. Although these methods possess good limit of detection and excellent sensitivity, they have a narrow linear range and D-PA was either determined by colorimetry or fluorescence measurement which confronts their use as a dual probe or sensor in practical applications. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is little attention has been paid to develop dual probe or sensors for the quantification of D-PA. So foremost aim of the present work is to improve the linear range, to develop a dual sensor for the detection of D-PA and to demonstrate the mechanism of detection in the system. To date, there are rare reports on the use of CDs as a dual probe for the determination of an analyte have been published. In this work, we report a novel strategy for the determination of D-PA in the pharmaceutical formulation by using carbon dots-iron (CDs-Fe3+) system with UV-visible spectroscopy and fluorescence technique. This CDs-Fe3+system not only conveniently employed as ‘turn on’ but also ‘turn off probe’ in fluorescence and UV-visible spectroscopy respectively for the determination of D-PA by dissociation of CDs-Fe3+ complex and formation of the stable D-PA-Fe3+ complex. The proposed method is less laborious, simple, flexible, cost effective and ecofriendly method.

All the reagents required for the experiment were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The used all metal salts were purchased from S d fine-chem. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). D-PA was procured from Sigma-Aldrich Ltd (Mumbai, India). All the other biomolecules used were also purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Ltd. The stock solutions of every compound were prepared by dissolving their corresponding compounds in doubly distilled water and used throughout the experiment.

The absorption spectra were recorded on UV Specord Analytik jena UV–VIS spectrophotometer at room temperature having 1.0 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence measurement was performed with Spectrofluorometer (JASCO Model FP–8300 Japan) with xenon arc lamp as a source with both excitation and emission slit was set at 5 nm bandpass. Time Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) Spectrophotometer HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH employing a nano second diode laser (IBH, nano LED-07) was used to measure fluorescence emission decay curves of the system. Zeta potential and Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements were obtained from Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) at room temperature. The morphology and size of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), which was performed on a JEOL-2100F instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kv.The crystallinity was characterized by powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, X-ray diffractometer) with CuKα1 (λ = 1.5406 A0) radiation with the voltage of 45 kV and current of 40 mÅ. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was employed to know the elemental composition of CDs. Cell imaging was carried out on Zeiss Axio-scope A 1 trinocular phase contrast fluorescent microscope at the different excitation wavelength.

The CDs were synthesized from Mahogany fruit shell as a precursor by using reported chemical oxidation method described by Z. A. Qiao et al. with some modification [42]. In a typical synthesis procedure, activated carbon was prepared from Mahogany fruit shell by the appropriate combination of dried powder of shell and concentration H2SO4 followed by neutralization. The 0.5 gm of activated carbon was dissolved in 50 mL of HNO3 (1 mol/L) solution and sonicated for 10 min. By refluxing a mixture for 12 h, the solution changed from colorless to brownish yellow indicating the formation of CDs. Further solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 15 min to remove deposit remained after the reflux. Then supernatant was collected and neutralized with Na2CO3 followed by filtration with 0.22 μm membrane to eliminate the larger particles. Finally, CDs solution was obtained by dialysis through dialysis membrane (MWCO of 1000 D) against double distilled water for 24 h and stored at 4 °C for further experiment.

Both UV-visible and FL measurements, for the detection of D-PA, were carried out at room temperature. Firstly, the effect of Fe3+ on the absorption and fluorescence intensity of CDs was studied. For these typical measurements, 0.5 mL of CDs solution and different amount of standard Fe3+solution was added into a standard 5 mL volumetric flask separately and then solutions were diluted with double distilled water up to the mark. Subsequently, the solutions were mixed thoroughly and allowed to react at room temperature for 10 min. Finally, the whole set of solutions were subjected to FL measurements.

Afterward, an optimum CDs-Fe3+ system was fabricated with an appropriate concentration of Fe3+ (12 μg mL−1) and CDs (1.84 mg mL−1) which has been further used as a dual probe for the detection of D-PA. In connection to this, a known amount of D-PA solution was serially added to the above system in such a way that the final concentration of D-PA ranged from 0 to 80 μg mL−1. The solutions were equilibrated for 10 min at room temperature and subjected to both UV- Visible and FL measurement. The selectivity of this developed probe was carried out by using stock solutions of different biomolecules, cations, and anions in a similar fashion. The fluorescence spectra were measured at 320 nm as an excitation wavelength with slit width 5 nm for both excitation and emission with medium intensity. The absorption bands of the solutions were recorded in the wavelength ranging from 200 nm to 450 nm by UV-visible spectrophotometer and the maximum absorption peak at 300 nm was used for quantitative analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of D-PA in pharmaceutical sample
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze D-PA in pharmaceutical preparations, a capsule, D-PA as the main content, was dissolved in double distilled water and sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, the whole solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm PVDF membrane filter and diluted with distilled water to the suitable concentration. This solution was further diluted to make the concentration of D-PA within the working range. Thus, the as prepared D-PA solution was added to the optimized CD-Fe3+ system to record the fluorescence as well as UV-visible spectrum. From this spectrum quantification of D-PA in given sample were carried out with the standard addition method.

The S. cerevisiae yeast strain was grown in peptone-dextrose agar (PDA) medium (pH 6.5 ± 0.2) at 30 °C. The growth of cells was controlled by measuring the optical density at specific wavelength. For cell imaging experiment, cell fixation was carried out with 70% (v/v) ethanol at 4 °C for 5 min. Furthermore, these cells were resuspended in phosphate buffer and incubated with 20 μL of carbon dot solution at room temperature for 3 h. The treated cell culture was washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and finally, sample was subjected to confocal microscopy at three different excitation wavelengths.

In cell cytotoxicity study, yeast cells were inoculated with varying concentration of CDs in PDA medium in 150 mL Erlenmeyer flasks. These flasks were incubated stationary at 37 °Cfor24 h. Furthermore, cell viability was interpreted by measuring optical density at regular time intervals on a spectrophotometer (at 620 nm). Each experiment was repeated twice and their replicate values were used to demonstrate cell viability of CDs.

The CDs was prepared from activated carbon, made from Mahogany fruit shell, by chemical oxidation method. The synthesized CDs have been confirmed by the various characterization techniques. The optical properties of CDs were explored by the use of UV-Visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. In UV-Visible absorption spectrum, as prepared CDs absorbs in the UV region with a tail extending in the visible region. The CDs display absorption shoulder at 300 nm which is ascribed to the carbonyl functional group at the surface and n-π* or π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [24][Fig. 1 ]. The FL spectra, fascinating feature, of CDs exhibit emission at 430 nm with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) is 79.80 nm which indicates narrow size distribution of CDs. The fluorescence of CDs might be due to surface defects and different functional group present on the surface of CDs [43]. Furthermore, FL spectrum of CDs was recorded with varying excitation wavelength from 290 nm–350 nm and highest emission intensity centered at 430 nm upon 320 nm excitation reveal it as a proper excitation wavelength of CDs [Fig. 1b]. The quantum yield of synthesized CDs was determined by the comparative method. It was found to be 1.9 %.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used for the determination of shape and particle size of the synthesized CDs. Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of the CDs, depicts spherically shaped and homogeneously distributed CDs with average particle size is 2.3 nm. The corresponding particle size distribution graph shows the diameter of CDs is in the range of 0.5–8 nm (Inset of Fig. 1a). Moreover, crystallinity of synthesized CDs was studied by XRD spectra. The typical XRD pattern illustrates that the broad diffraction peak is centered at 2θ = 22.540 indicates poor crystallinity or amorphous nature of CDs [Fig. 2b] [44]. EDX analysis suggested the presences of carbon (C), oxygen (O) nitrogen (N) and sodium (Na) in the synthesized CDs nanomaterials. [Fig. S1]. The sodium impurity might be due to the Na2CO3 which was employed in the neutralization process.

The effect of pH on the luminescence property of CDs was investigated with buffer solutions with different pH ranging from 2 to 12. Fig. 3 a depicts the fluorescence behavior of CDs with different pH; an almost no change in emission intensity of CDs with pH observed which indicates pH independent nature of CDs. The results reveal that as synthesized CDs is highly stable in whole pH range.

Apart from the excellent luminescence stability of CDs with different pH solutions, it was also found that emission intensity response of CDs remained unaffected over the concentration range of NaCl 0.1–1.0 M Fig. 3b. This salt independent fluorescence of CDs suggested that it is highly stable in extreme ionic strength solution which would provide a space for CDs to use as a probe in highly ionic solutions.

Initially, CDs, a base material of fluorescent probe, exhibited marvelous photoluminescence at 430 when excited at 320 nm. Then the response of highly fluorescent of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ was measured with the addition of a different amount of Fe3+ (0–28 μg mL−1) to the aqueous solution of CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased substantially within 5 min revealed that system was sensitive to Fe3+ concentration [Fig. S2]. This observation ascribed to the fact that Fe3+ has a strong binding affinity to the functionalized surface of CDs leading to the formation CDs-Fe3+ complex while there was no any notable change in fluorescence of CDs, compared to Fe3+, in presence of different fifteen metal ions [Fig. S3]. Therefore, there was scope to select Fe3+ion, as it shows better quenching efficiency, for further study. The fluorescence intensity of CDs quenched with Fe3+was well analyzed by the standard Stern-Volmer equation.
(1) F0/F = 1+KSV [Q]

Where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without quencher, F is the observed fluorescence intensity in the presence of the quencher, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher Fe3+. A typical Stern-Volmer plot, F0/F versus [Q], shown in Fig. S4 disobeys linear response of intensity to the Fe3+concentration over the whole 0−28 μg mL−1concentration range. In contrast, inset of Fig. S4 exhibited good linear calibration between 0 and 12 μg mL−1concentration with excellent correlation coefficient and limit of detection (LOD). As the intensity linearly correlates with the variation in the analyte concentration, we have chosen 12 μg mL−1 of Fe3+as an optimum concentration for subsequent study. Furthermore, Fig. 4 depicted the fluorescent intensity of CDs has not affected by the D-PA which clearly indicated that there was a rare possibility of complex formation or any other processes like energy transfer or charge transfer between them. But after the introduction of D-PA in CDs-Fe3+ system an almost 76.4% recovery of emission intensity of CDs was noticed. From this recovery, it was observed that CDs-Fe3+ system could find a new avenue for the determination of D-PA.

The surface functionalized carbonyl group is primarily responsible for absorption shoulder at 300 nm of CDs. With the addition of 12 μgmL−1 of Fe3+solutions to the dispersion of CDs, an absorbance of the CDs drastically increased which is again attributed to the interaction of surface functional groups with Fe3+results the formation of CDs-Fe3+complex. However, CDs-D-PA system did not show any change in absorption spectra which confirmed less possibility of complex formation between them. The D-PA-Fe3+ complex also not absorbed in the same region which rules out the probability of absorption by D-PA-Fe3+complex [Fig. 5 ]. Thus, selectively decrease in fluorescence and increased absorbance of CDs in presence of Fe3+and highly chelating ability and affinity of D-PA towards Fe3+ inspired us to use this manipulated CDs-Fe3+ system as a dual probe for the selective determination of D-PA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-Fe3+ system based “turn-on” fluorescence detection of D-PA
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the selectivity of a proposed CDs-Fe3+ system for the D-PA over the other interfering substances like glutathione (GSH), thiourea, cystamine hydrochloride, glucose, dopamine, d-tyrosine, glycine, urea, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cl− etc. were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8 , merely D-PA could enhance the fluorescence signal of a CDs-Fe3+ system while other substances were unable to produce a considerable effect on fluorescence intensity. Thus, the excellent sensitivity, wider linear range, low cost, simple proposed method paves the way for the efficient quantification of D-PA in real samples by spectrofluorometrically.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-Fe3+ system based “turn-off” UV-Visible detection of D-PA
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption at 300 nm of CDs has been considerably enhanced after the addition of Fe3+ owing to the complex formation between surface functionalities of CDs and ion. D-PA also has strong binding ability to Fe3+and in presence of D-PA interaction between Fe3+ and CDs is perturbed and removed from the surface of CDs [45,46]. Thus, as shown in Fig. 9 the absorbance intensity of CDs-Fe3+ system is decreased with gradual increase in concentration of D-PA with noticeable shift in absorption wavelength. Under the experimental condition the absorbance of CDs-Fe3+system was plotted against the concentration of D-PA to obtain the linear relationship. Fig. 10 shows equilibration curve of (A0-A) of CDs-Fe3+system against amount of D-PA with excellent linearity within the range of 0–40 μg mL−1 and linearity equation is (A0-A) = 0.0177x with coefficient of determination is 0.989. The limit of detection of the proposed CDs-Fe3+system as a UV probe for D-PA calculated and found to be 5.86 μg mL−1.

The performance of the developed system has been evaluated by the selectivity study. Therefore, to address the selectivity of the developed sensor towards the D-PA various inferring substances, same as like in fluorescence study, like biomolecules and ions were evaluated under the identical experimental condition. As shown in Fig. 11 , the interferons, comparatively higher in concentration than D-PA, did not affect the absorption spectra of CDs-Fe3+system considerably while 40 μg mL−1of D-PA shows visible change in absorption spectra. Hence all these experimental observations prove that the developed CDs-Fe3+ based UV probe could also be act as a selective sensor for the quantification of D-PA.

The quenching of fluorescence intensity and enhancement in absorbance of CDs with the addition of Fe3+ ion could be attributed to the interaction between functional group present on the surface of CDs and Fe3+ion. These two processes might be due to the formation of non-fluorescent stable CDs-Fe3+ complex which is consistent with previously reported method [47]. The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism was evaluated by fluorescence lifetime experiment. The average fluorescence lifetime of CDs in presences of Fe3+ was changed from 2.30 ns to 2.14 ns which are nearly constant. This result reveals that static type of quenching, attributed to the formation of ground state non-fluorescent complex between carboxyl and hydroxyl functional group of CDs and Fe3+, is the driving force for the quenching of fluorescence [Fig. S5, Table S1]. Moreover, insight of quenching mechanism also studied by UV-absorption spectra. The change in absorption of CDs in presence of Fe3+ also supports a static type of quenching [Fig. S6]42. The blue shift in emission spectra [Fig. S2], change as well as shift in absorption spectrum and almost constant lifetime of CDs with Fe3+ confirms, quenching of the emission intensity provoked by Fe3+is likely the static type. However, strong affinity of D-PA towards Fe3+preferentially forms stable complex. Thus, the quenched fluorescence of CDs-Fe3+system is recovered. The Fig. 4 shows that Fe3+-D-PA system didn't have any fluorescence in the emission range of the CDs which again confirms removal of Fe3+from the surface of CDs; the main reason for fluorescence restoration. Meanwhile, the absorption intensity of CDs-Fe3+ system in presence D-PA is lowered [Fig. 5] which indicated that D-PA disturbs the complex owing to the robust binding ability of D-PA to Fe3+. Thus, formation of D-PA-Fe3+ complex was responsible for changes in absorption spectrum again [Fig. 5].

So, in order to get further insight of the mechanism of fluorescence recovery and decrease in absorbance of CDs-Fe3+ system by D-PA, experiment like zeta potential and DLS measurement has been carried out. As shown in Fig. 12 CDs have −23.14 mV potential since it bears negatively charged hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on their surface. After the addition of 12.0 μg mL−1of Fe3+ solution, the potential was changed from −23.14 to −8.9 mV indicates an association of functional group of CDs and positively charged Fe3+ ion. Whereas zeta potential after the introduction of D-PA to the above system decreased again up to −19.6 mV due to the strong chelating capability of D-PA which removes Fe3+ from the surface of CDs and eventually fluorescence restoration and decrease in absorbance was observed. This observation is also further supported by DLS measurement. The average particle size of the CDs determined by the DLS was found to be 38.5 nm since it measures the hydrodynamic diameter. It was increased up to the 113.5 nm by the addition of Fe3+ ion, which suggested that there is aggregation between CDs and Fe3+ ion owing to the complex formation between them. Furthermore, particle size was significantly decreased from 113.5 to 66.9 nm with the addition of D-PA indicated a perturbation of CD-Fe3+ complex [Fig. S7]. FT-IR spectra also used to get idea about the complex formation between CDs and Fe3+ ion. Fig. S8 indicates change in peak shape of hydroxyl as well as carbonyl after addition of Fe3+ in CDs solution reasonably due to the complex formation between them.

Herein the applicability of fabricated CDs-Fe3+ system was assessed for the determination of D-PA in pharmaceutical samples by both spectroscopic techniques. By following the procedure mentioned in the section in 2.5, the contents of the D-PA were determined by the standard addition method. The results are summarized in Table 1 . It shows excellent recoveries, ranging from 98.46 to 99.72% and relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3.24% indicated good precision of the developed method. Thus, these results revealed the trustworthiness of the proposed sensor system and provided an easy platform for the selective monitoring of D-PA in pharmaceutical sample by both the technique.

The bioimaging ability of CDs was evaluated with S. cerevisiae strain. Fig. 13 shows confocal fluorescence microscopic images S. cerevisiae yeast cell of under different excitation wavelengths at 405, 488, and 561 nm. The yeast cells treated with CDs (20 μL) show obvious blue, green, and red emissions after the 3 h incubation. These images indicate that CDs are widely dispersed and appeared in the membrane and cytoplasmic area of yeast cells. This is possible because of the cell permeability to the CDs. These results illustrate that CDs can be effectively applied for cell internalization and are able to serve as multiple fluorescence emission for in vivo cell imaging and biological labeling study.

Cell toxicity of CDs was measured in terms of optical density of strain, shown in Fig. 14 . Various concentration of CDs (0, 50, 100, 200, 400 μL) were incubated with yeast cells in orbital shaker at 37 °C. The growth of yeast strains with CDs was observed as like untreated strain (control) curves. These results indicate that, CDs did not show any considerable inhibition in growth of the test yeast strains during 24 h incubation. These studies demonstrate that, CDs synthesized from mahogany fruit shell exhibited good biocompatibility at all concentrations which open new avenues for novel biological applications.

In summary, we have synthesized highly fluorescent carbon dots from activated carbon derived from mahogany fruit shell by chemical oxidation method. The as prepared CDs demonstrated robust fluorescence quenching to Fe3+ ion over the several metals via formation of non-fluorescent stable ground state complex; static quenching mechanism type. Meanwhile, such a complex formation leads to an increase of absorption intensity of CDs with Fe3+.After the addition of D-PA, into CD-Fe3+ system, as expected, led to the retrieval of fluorescence as well as decrease in absorption due to the greater attraction of D-PA for Fe3+ which removes Fe3+ from the surface of CDs and forms strong bond with D-PA via thiol group. Therefore CD-Fe3+ system used as dual probe for the recognition of D-PA based on ‘turn-on’ and ‘turn-off’ strategy in fluorescence and UV-visible spectroscopy respectively. This developed method acts as single shot dual sensing probe for D-PA. Moreover, the synthesized CDs showed excellent biocompatibility and cell permeability hence found applicability for the bioimaging of yeast S. cerevisiae cells.
We wish to confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this Publication.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the author DBG is grateful to the University Grants Commission (UGC), New Delhi, India for providing financial assistance in the form of UGC-NET-JRF scheme (F.17-11/2008(SA-I). We are also thankful to the Shivaji University, Kolhapur for providing all necessary facilities for this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
supple supple

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.11.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: D-penicillamine adsorption on Gold: an in situ ATR-IR spectroscopic and QCM study
Penicillamine, a new oral therapy for Wilson's disease
